text: Hello, I'm showing a sigmoid resection for a colovacicular fistula. This is a patient who had a recent endovascular AAA and common iliac artery aneurysm repair, and has a history of a radical left nephrectomy for a cancer. So fortunately there's no ureter on the left hand side, but unfortunately it was a very scarred case.

I left the dissection of the fistula largely unedited because I wanted you to see the struggles and the principles of work were easy. And the rest of the case is otherwise pretty standard for how a sigmoid goes.

The surgeon navigates through extensive scarring in the left retroperitoneum, resulting from previous radical node dissection along the iliac vessels. Lateral dissection is performed to identify a potential plane down the left pelvic side wall into the gutter, but the surgeon decides to directly address the fistula due to difficulty in visualizing the plane.

The surgeon encounters the colon adhered extensively to the visceral surface of the bladder. Using cautery, the surgeon carefully dissects along the colon to restore normal anatomy, aiming to separate the adhered tissues and expose the pelvis while avoiding damage to the bladder.

The surgeon identifies the fistula connecting the colon to the bladder, noting a small intramural abscess at the site. The visible defect is highlighted as the surgeon attempts to dissect below the fistula, aiming to reveal free space in the pelvic region.

Nothing glamorous about these cases, just stay focused, don't lose your anatomy, and don't work in deep holes that are filled with blood. Just keep moving around, good use of suction to clear the blood and smoke, and there's a blunt dissector, and just keep slowly working.

Trying to work down the right pelvis. Didn't really like what I was seeing, so come back over to the left. The tensile strength of the robotic instruments I do feel has an advantage over that for straight stick laparoscopy. Simply for the reduction in the tension of the instruments in your hands, which can be very taxing and straining and uncomfortable over the course of a long surgery with tissue like this.

Nonetheless, these cases are absolutely done doable laparoscopically. And I don't think that the simple development of a fistula or the knowledge that a case would look like this should sway somebody away from doing laparoscopic surgery. I think that these patients tend to benefit the most for minimally invasive surgery.

The surgeon maintains meticulous hemostasis while observing that the colon is extensively adhered to the bladder. The pelvis is not visible at this point. The surgeon moves back over the pelvic brim to the left, where the common iliac artery is visible, and identifies a small window to access the pelvis. The ureter is not a concern as it has been removed.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection in the scarred plane between the colon and the left pelvic sidewall, attempting to open up the area anterior to the sigmoid colon into the pelvis. The tissue remains tightly fused, making progress challenging, as the surgeon navigates from a known window into an unknown space.

The surgeon moves to the right pelvis, gaining better access to detach the colon from the back of the bladder, where it is chronically attached. Pus is visible emerging from an intramural abscess, indicating a different location than previously observed.

The surgeon navigates below the colon, observing oozing from the surgical site. Robotic suction is utilized to manage fluid accumulation, highlighting its effectiveness in maintaining a clear view during the procedure.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the colon, noting the absence of retroperitoneal fat due to a previous nephrectomy. The colon is mobilized towards the splenic flexure to ensure a tension-free anastomosis. The surgeon checks the length, confirming it reaches without issue. A low ligation is performed, hugging the bowel wall, and the mesentery is dissected from proximal to distal, marked at the distal margin to maintain orientation.

The surgeon uses a stapler to transect the bowel at the distal margin. The camera view shifts as the staple port is inserted through the right rectus, aiming to minimize post-operative pain compared to the right lower quadrant approach.

The surgeon lines up the anastomosis, checking perfusion by observing the green enhancement of the tissue lip. The posterior staple lines are joined using TuoPDS sutures. The surgeon sews one side, brings another suture from the middle, and then sews the other side, demonstrating the end cannelling over the top.

Overall, I think these cases can take people out of their comfort zone because of the loss of the normal anatomy down in the pelvis, often not a lot of working room, and a feeling of insecurity and dissecting in certain locations. Difficult pelvis cases are obviously very challenging and difficult, but we all get them, diverticular disease being ever so common and probably the most frequent. reason for complex sigmoid surgery that I do.

But once you get that part out of the equation, the rest of the case is exactly the same and outcomes should be expected to be exactly the same. I had no concerns over what I saw with the fistula and the healthiness of both the proximal and distal margin to go ahead and make an anastomosis. I would never tell a person like this that I think that they should be diverted from the outset

The surgeon completes the anterior portion of a two-layer anastomosis using TuoPDS sutures, taking seromuscular bites around the bowel. The suture is tied to the original posterior TuoPDS suture post. Preparations are made for a leak test, including testing the bladder.

The surgeon performs a leak test on the colorectal anastomosis using methylene blue and saline, filling the bladder up to 400 cc's to check for leaks. Despite the presence of a small intramural abscess, no leakage is observed, indicating no need for a drain.
